brief: GAP/WHY: Current guidelines do not endorse smartphone photoplethysmography (PPG) for diagnosing atrial fibrillation (AF) without a confirmatory ECG. Prior validation studies were done supervised in healthcare settings, limiting real-world generalizability. Need: assess unsupervised ambulatory smartphone-PPG performance including atrial flutter (AFL) patients. DESIGN/SETTING: Prospective diagnostic validation study; real-world, ambulatory, unsupervised home recordings; PPG compared head-to-head with simultaneous single-lead ECG as reference. POPULATION/N: Unselected patients undergoing direct current cardioversion for AF or AFL; total 280 patients; 18 005 simultaneous PPG+ECG recordings analyzed. PROTOCOL/INTERVENTION: Patients performed 1-min heart rhythm recordings post-cardioversion, at least twice daily for 30 days at home. Device under test = CORAI Heart Monitor PPG app on iPhone 7, run simultaneously with single-lead ECG (KardiaMobile). Both PPG and ECG recordings read independently by two experienced human readers (manual interpretation). KEY RESULTS (exact numbers): Sufficient diagnostic quality: PPG 96.9% vs ECG 95.1% of recordings (P<0.001), i.e. PPG quality higher. Manual PPG reading vs manually interpreted ECG -- WITH AFL recordings INCLUDED: sensitivity 97.7%, specificity 99.4%, overall accuracy 98.9%. WITH AFL recordings EXCLUDED: sensitivity 99.0%, specificity 99.7%, accuracy 99.5%. TAKEAWAY: A novel smartphone-PPG method, used unsupervised by patients at home, achieves accurate AF and AFL rhythm diagnostics with very high sensitivity and specificity; can serve as a standalone heart-rhythm diagnostic device after cardioversion without requiring ECG confirmation.
